title: Where Numbers Wash into Salt

features.name: the dissolving cipher
features.desc-on-touch: You read a tide table printed in salt, numbers bleeding into the margin where foam still remembers the shape of measurement.

features.name: the overflow ledger
features.desc-on-touch: A filecard listing water heights, each entry more salt-worn than the last, the numbers themselves starting to change.

features.name: the pooling edge
features.desc-on-touch: Where the estuary's water meets a reflecting pool of brine, coordinates written on the water's own surface.

features.name: the salt-worn beacon
features.desc-on-touch: A wireframe tower that once held measurement instruments; they have rusted into sculptures of what they used to know.

features.name: the threshold where counting ends
features.desc-on-touch: A place where the tide chart becomes a poem; where the archive stops numbering and starts listening to the salt.

inscription: where the tide gives up its grid and teaches the salt to read in wavelengths instead of numbers

file.name: TideTable_Estuarine_LastMeasurements_Corrupted.txt
file.note: Recorded water heights and salinity from 1982–1998; the numbers have begun to drift, each copy slightly more salt-worn than the archive's backup.

exits.label: back along the shore where numbers have become foam
westward where salt takes its own measure
upriver where the estuary narrows and remembers it was a stream
